verses.text: A prophet's widow, hounded by a creditor who would take her sons as slaves, cries to Elisha, who asks what she has — only a pot of oil. God begins with the little already in her hand. He often works His deliverance through the small resource we think too slight to matter.

A prophet's widow, hounded by a creditor who would take her sons as slaves, cries to Elisha, who asks what she has — only a pot of oil. God begins with the little already in her hand. He often works His deliverance through the small resource we think too slight to matter.

She borrows many vessels, shuts the door, and pours; the oil flows till every jar is full, then stops. She sells it, pays the debt, and lives on the rest. God's supply fills exactly the room we make for it by faith; the limit was never His oil but her empty jars.

She borrows many vessels, shuts the door, and pours; the oil flows till every jar is full, then stops. She sells it, pays the debt, and lives on the rest. God's supply fills exactly the room we make for it by faith; the limit was never His oil but her empty jars.

A wealthy woman of Shunem perceives Elisha is a holy man and builds him a little chamber for his visits. She gives, expecting nothing. Quiet, thoughtful hospitality to God's servants is a ministry He notices and remembers.

A wealthy woman of Shunem perceives Elisha is a holy man and builds him a little chamber for his visits. She gives, expecting nothing. Quiet, thoughtful hospitality to God's servants is a ministry He notices and remembers.

A wealthy woman of Shunem perceives Elisha is a holy man and builds him a little chamber for his visits. She gives, expecting nothing. Quiet, thoughtful hospitality to God's servants is a ministry He notices and remembers.

Elisha offers to speak for her to the king, but she answers, 'I dwell among mine own people' — content, asking no favour. Her service was not angling for reward. True generosity gives freely, neither expecting nor seeking repayment for its kindness.

Elisha offers to speak for her to the king, but she answers, 'I dwell among mine own people' — content, asking no favour. Her service was not angling for reward. True generosity gives freely, neither expecting nor seeking repayment for its kindness.

Learning she is childless and her husband old, Elisha promises a son within the year, and despite her protest, the child is born. God gives the very thing she had stopped daring to hope for. He delights to grant the deep, unspoken longing of a faithful heart.

Years later the boy cries 'My head,' and dies at noon on his mother's knees; she lays him on the prophet's bed and shuts the door. Her promised joy becomes sudden grief. The gifts God gives are still held in His hands, and faith is tested even in its blessings.

Telling her husband only that all is well, she rides hard to Mount Carmel to find the man of God. She does not despair or wail but acts, carrying her grief straight to the LORD's servant. Faith in trouble moves toward God, not away from Him.

Telling her husband only that all is well, she rides hard to Mount Carmel to find the man of God. She does not despair or wail but acts, carrying her grief straight to the LORD's servant. Faith in trouble moves toward God, not away from Him.

Telling her husband only that all is well, she rides hard to Mount Carmel to find the man of God. She does not despair or wail but acts, carrying her grief straight to the LORD's servant. Faith in trouble moves toward God, not away from Him.

To every question she answers 'It is well,' until she reaches Elisha and falls at his feet: did I ask for a son? Her calm breaks into honest anguish before the prophet. Real faith can hold both quiet trust and raw, open grief at the same time.

Elisha sends Gehazi ahead with his staff to lay on the child, but it does not wake him. Method and instrument without the prophet's own presence and prayer accomplish nothing. The form of power is empty where the living touch of God is absent.

Elisha sends Gehazi ahead with his staff to lay on the child, but it does not wake him. Method and instrument without the prophet's own presence and prayer accomplish nothing. The form of power is empty where the living touch of God is absent.

Elisha sends Gehazi ahead with his staff to lay on the child, but it does not wake him. Method and instrument without the prophet's own presence and prayer accomplish nothing. The form of power is empty where the living touch of God is absent.

Elisha shuts himself in, prays, and stretches himself upon the child twice, until the boy's flesh warms and he sneezes seven times and opens his eyes. Life returns through earnest, persistent prayer. The servant gives himself wholly to the work God will do through him.

Elisha calls the mother, and she takes up her living son, falling at his feet in speechless gratitude. The grief that drove her to Carmel is turned to joy. God restores, and the faith that would not let go is rewarded beyond its asking.

Elisha calls the mother, and she takes up her living son, falling at his feet in speechless gratitude. The grief that drove her to Carmel is turned to joy. God restores, and the faith that would not let go is rewarded beyond its asking.

In a famine the prophets' stew is poisoned by wild gourds — 'death in the pot' — but Elisha casts in meal and it is made safe. A deadly mistake is healed by God's provision. He guards His servants even from the harm their own hands unwittingly prepare.

A man brings twenty barley loaves, and Elisha bids them feed a hundred; they eat and leave some over, 'according to the word of the LORD.' Little, blessed by God, more than suffices — a foreshadow of the greater feeding by David's Son, who multiplies what is offered Him.
